canto_title: The Vision of the Eternal Light and the Beatific Vision

scenes.summary: Dante offers a formal theological praise of Mary, celebrating her role in the Incarnity and her boundless grace.
Dante and Saint Bernard ask Mary to intercede for the removal of all clouds from the soul to reach the ultimate salvation.
The eyes of the Virgin, beloved by God, turn toward the praying Dante to show their grace.
As Dante reaches the end of all earthly desire, his vision enters the supreme, true light.
The overwhelming nature of the vision causes Dante's memory and vision to fade like a dream or melting snow.
Dante prays that the light will grant him enough power to leave a spark of its glory for future generations.
The extreme brightness of the divine light causes Dante's vision to be consumed by its brilliance.
Within the depths of the light, Dante perceives the underlying unity of all substances and accidents in the universe.
Dante recognizes that all goodness and will are contained within the light, making anything outside of it defective.
Dante struggles to describe the vision because the light changes its appearance as his own perception is transformed.
Dante perceives three rings of different colors revolving within the profound clarity of the light.
Dante laments that his words are too feeble and brief to capture the magnitude of what he has seen.
Dante contemplates the Eternal Light that exists, understands, and loves itself in perfect solitude.
The circulation of color within the light appears to Dante as a reflection of the human image.
Like a geometer unable to find the center of a circle, Dante's intellect fails to grasp the infinite scope of the vision.
The human intellect is struck by a divine flash, aligning Dante's desire with the love that moves the universe.
